column12: Making it draggable might not be that intuitive. If let's say you have a very big range (eg 1e23) and a small step size, the "drag" might need to be a lot more to see the value change especially when we combine it with precision which shows only a few numbers
column22: Again, similar point to last, also some confusion about which "t" will it point to if 2 of them exist in the formula, which is often the case

column12: It was relatively harder to set/access complex variables' value in the semantics function. In particular, it was not very intuitive in the sense that it was not immediately obvious where in the chronology of variable assignment should the output variable go. Also, it would be helpful to have a way to target a given occurrence if a variable occurs multiple times within a formula.
column22: The semantics function does not function as a "one to one" with its "math formula counterpart, since you need to compute some values prior to assigning an output variable (in the case you have an output variable). As a result, knowing what to calculate and access before the final assignment is made can be a slight drag.

column12: It was very easy to define teh variables and use them in formula and render
column22: It was Important to pay attention to the way the formula was accessing special variables like lambda, other pieces were relatively straightforward.

column12: It is a bit hard to get the interactive formula working EXACTLY the way I want it, like with the placement of tags (for example when I had two t's in the formula, it automatically put the label on the first tag and i don't think there was a way to change that, or for example choosing between the labels being at the top or at the bottom, or what if wanted them somewhere else like on the side?)
I think the range and step size + sig figs parameters needed some thinking - but other than that pretty straightforward.
For time, only one of them was labeled i.e. N(t), not the t in the right hand side of the formula
column22: Most of the API was very intuitive and easy to follow, and very similar to the tutorial.

column12: Creating interactive formulae is very intutive with a config setup. Having variables defined in the same place makes editing simple and gives you visibility of everything you have created in one place. Adding multiple formulae is more tricky though!
column22: Again, having all the variable definitions in one place makes setting all these labels, ranges etc. very simple.

column12: For the 4th one, I don't think the definition of significant figures used in the library correlates to what the normal conception (or at least my conception) of significant figures. For instance, sigFigs = 3 of 0.000121 should be 0 (0.000), not 0.000121, because the normal convention is 3 significant digits after 0.
column22: Thinking of a brief and readable label could be hard but it is less relevant to the library.

column12: I think it's a little confusing how if a variable is defined using complex LaTeX then you have to wrap it in brackets and quotations but it's not super impairing or limiting
column22: I don't really have many complaints about the library, I think it was fairly easy to set up and make the formula interactive

column12: Expressing the computation with a semantics function was not the most intutive if i was trying to calcularte a varible in the function, rather than the left hand side variable
column22: Accessing a variable's value in a semantics function difficult if wasn't simple variable or variable with subscript and ndeeded to use bracket notation
